module-7-clean-student-records.label: Module Project: Clean Student Records
module-7-clean-student-records.summary: A student group sends you a messy file of records. Build a cleaner that reads the file, handles bad rows, and writes a usable output.

sketch0.title: A messy student file lands in your inbox

mp_3.title: Skip bad rows and collect records

mp-1-read-student-file.title: Read the student file
mp-1-read-student-file.prompt: A student group emailed you `data/students.csv` and asked what is inside it. Read the file and print every raw row without extra blank lines so you can inspect the data before cleaning it.
mp-1-read-student-file.hint: Open the file, loop through each line, and print the stripped line.

help.concept: A real cleaning job usually starts by inspecting the raw file before changing anything.
help.hint_2: Inside the loop, print `line.strip()` to avoid extra blank lines.

mp-1-read-student-file.starterCode: # Read data/students.csv and print every line without extra blank lines.

mp-2-clean-one-row.title: Clean and validate one row
mp-2-clean-one-row.prompt: Now clean one record at a time. Write `clean_row(line)` so it splits a CSV row into name and score, strips spaces, title-cases the name, converts the score to an integer, and prints cleaned dictionaries while showing `bad row` for invalid rows.
mp-2-clean-one-row.hint: Make one function responsible for turning raw text into one clean dictionary.

help.concept: A cleaning function gives the rest of the program one dependable shape for each valid row.
help.hint_2: Use `try`/`except` around calls that might fail so invalid rows can show `bad row`.

mp-3-skip-bad-rows.title: Skip bad rows and collect records
mp-3-skip-bad-rows.prompt: The group only wants usable records in the final list. Start from `clean_row`, build a list named `records` containing only valid cleaned rows, and skip invalid rows without crashing.
mp-3-skip-bad-rows.hint: Use `try`/`except` inside the file loop and append only successful rows.

help.concept: In real data files, some rows are broken. A cleaner should keep good rows and quietly move past bad ones.
help.hint_2: Put `records.append(clean_row(line))` inside `try`, and use `continue` or `pass` in `except ValueError`.

mp-4-write-clean-records.title: Write cleaned records
mp-4-write-clean-records.prompt: The student group needs a clean file they can share. Start from the cleaned `records` list, write the valid records to `output/clean_students.csv`, and print how many clean records were written.
mp-4-write-clean-records.hint: Create the output file from the cleaned list, then print a short confirmation.

help.concept: The final step of a cleaning job is delivering a clean artifact, not just printing data to the screen.
help.hint_2: Write one cleaned record per line, then print the count of records written.

try-module-7-clean-student-records-sketc.title: Preview a cleaned row
try-module-7-clean-student-records-sketc.prompt: Clean one row before the full records project.
try-module-7-clean-student-records-sketc.hint: Use the TODO comments as a guide, then run the program and compare the output to the example.

help.concept: This Try it yourself checkpoint turns the lesson idea from "Clean student records step by step" with a small runnable program.
help.hint_1: Use the TODO comments as your checklist for this small job.
help.hint_2: Make sure the final program shows the result the scenario needs.

sketch-1.title: A messy student file lands in your inbox
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
A student group exported a file of names and scores, but some rows are messy and some are broken. They do not need a lecture about data cleaning; they need a small Python tool that reads the file, keeps the good records, skips the bad ones, and writes something usable.

This module project grows one careful step at a time. Each starter is the previous canonical solution plus the next realistic job.
